mission.title: Install and Configure Meshery on Kubernetes
mission.description: Meshery is the cloud-native manager that helps you manage service meshes across various environments. Installing Meshery allows you to visualize, manage, and optimize your service mesh implementations effectively.

steps.title: Add the Meshery Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Meshery Helm chart repository to your local Helm setup using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add meshery https://meshery.io/charts
```

steps.title: Install Meshery using Helm
steps.description:
Install Meshery in a new namespace called 'meshery' with the following command:
```bash
helm install meshery meshery/meshery --namespace meshery --create-namespace --version v0.8.205
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Meshery pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace meshery
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the Meshery UI
steps.description:
Forward the Meshery service to your local machine to access the UI:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/meshery --namespace meshery 9081:9081
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Meshery will show all pods in the 'meshery' namespace as running. You can access the Meshery UI by navigating to http://localhost:9081 in your web browser.
codeSnippets: helm install meshery meshery/meshery --namespace meshery --create-namespace --version v0.8.205

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Meshery installation, first uninstall the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall meshery --namespace meshery
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Meshery:
```bash
kubectl delete crd mesheryconfigs.meshery.io
kubectl delete crd mesherypatterns.meshery.io
kubectl delete crd mesheryservices.meshery.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace meshery
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'meshery' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace meshery
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Meshery installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace meshery -o yaml > meshery-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Meshery to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade meshery meshery/meshery --namespace meshery --version v0.8.206
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Meshery pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace meshery
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see that the Meshery pods are in a 'CrashLoopBackOff' state, check the logs for the specific pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace meshery
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Meshery UI, ensure that the service is running and the port-forwarding command is active:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace meshery
```
If the service is not running, check the pod status and logs for issues.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource usage of the Meshery pods:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace meshery
```
Consider adjusting the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails, check the output for specific error messages. You can also check the current Helm release status:
```bash
helm status meshery --namespace meshery
```
If needed, you can roll back to the previous version:
```bash
helm rollback meshery <revision-number> --namespace meshery
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
